name: Handwoven Bamboo Basket Set (3 pieces)
description: Three nesting baskets woven from Muli bamboo harvested from community-managed groves in Bongaigaon. The Mishing tribe has practiced this weaving technique for over 800 years. Each basket uses a different weave pattern — the hexagonal twill for the largest, the diamond herringbone for the medium, and the plain over-under for the smallest. No synthetic dyes or chemical treatments are used. The bamboo is smoked over paddy husk fires for 48 hours to waterproof naturally.

name: Longpi Black Pottery Bowl
description: Longpi ham (black stone pottery) is made exclusively in Longpi village by the Tangkhul Naga community using a unique combination of two locally found materials — serpentinite stone powder and iron-rich clay. No potter's wheel is used. The vessel is shaped entirely by hand against a banana leaf. After shaping, it is fired at low temperature in an open-air kiln using rice husks and then burnished with tamarind bark to create the distinctive non-porous black surface. The result is naturally non-stick and safe for direct flame.

name: Dhokra Brass Elephant Figurine
certifications: ["GI Tag Bastar Dhokra", "Women Artisan", "Tribal Craft Certified"]
description: Cast using the 4,000-year-old lost-wax (cire perdue) method by Sonabai Sori of the Ghadwa community in Kondagaon. A wax model is sculpted over a clay core, then coated in clay and fired — the melting wax is replaced by molten brass. The distinctive granular texture of Dhokra work comes from the coiled wax threads applied to the model before firing. This elephant stands 22 cm tall and depicts a ceremonial howdah with geometric tribal motifs that reference the Gondi cosmological system.

name: Warli Hand-painted Storage Trunk
description: A wooden storage trunk painted entirely in traditional Warli style by apprentices of the Mashe workshop in Talasari — the home village of Jivya Soma Mashe, the artist who first brought Warli art to international attention in the 1970s. The paintings use only rice paste (white) on a terracotta-red mud background, depicting the Tarpa dance, farming cycles, and the tree of life. The trunk itself is made from mango wood salvaged from fallen trees in the Jawhar forest.

name: Terracotta Wind Chime — Bankura Horses
description: A wind chime featuring five miniature Bankura horses — the iconic terracotta horses offered at the Bishnupur temples for over 300 years. Made by Tapan Kumar Kumbhakar whose family has potted in Panchmura for seven generations. The elongated neck and upward-turned ears of the Bankura horse are its defining features, shaped on a wheel and finished by hand. Fired in a community kiln using biomass fuel, then given a natural red oxide wash. The chime produces tones in the range of 440-880 Hz.

name: Organic Nilgiri Orthodox Tea (250g)
description: Single-origin Orthodox tea from Glenmore Estate at 1,800 metres in the Nilgiri hills. Orthodox processing preserves the full leaf intact — unlike CTC (crush-tear-curl) which shreds the leaf — producing a complex, layered cup. This tea is shade-grown under a canopy of silver oak and grevillea trees that also serve as bird habitat. The muscatel character (a grape-honey note unique to Nilgiri teas) comes from the leaf-hopper insect bite — so no pesticides can be used without destroying the flavour. Packaged in compostable kraft paper with a paper inner liner.

name: Coorg Single-Origin Filter Coffee (250g)
description: A medium-dark roast from a single estate in Coorg, Karnataka — Coffea arabica grown under a canopy of silver oak at 1,100 metres. The beans are sun-dried on raised African beds for 25 days (natural/dry process) which imparts a distinctly fruity, wine-like body. Ground to the traditional South Indian filter coffee consistency — coarser than espresso, finer than pour-over. Roasted in small batches in Madikeri using a biomass-fired drum roaster. The Kodava Coffee Collective pays 30% above Fair Trade minimum price to all member farmers.

name: Sundarbans Organic Wild Forest Honey (500g)
description: Wild forest honey collected by the Moule (honey hunters) of the Sundarbans — men who wade into the mangrove forests during the honey season (April-June) to harvest from Apis dorsata (rock bee) colonies in the tree canopy. The Moule wear traditional smoky torches but no protective gear — a spiritual tradition involves prayers to Banbibi, the goddess of the forest, before any harvest. This honey is raw, unheated, unfiltered — strained only through a cloth mesh. The flavour profile is intensely floral from Sundari and Gewa tree blossoms, with a distinct salinity from sea air. Certified organic because no agriculture exists within 20 km.

name: Manipuri Black Rice — Chakhao Amubi (1kg)
description: Chakhao Amubi (black glutinous rice) is unique to Manipur and holds a GI (Geographical Indication) tag. The intense purple-black colour comes from anthocyanins — the same antioxidant pigment found in blueberries. It is served exclusively at ceremonial feasts in Meitei culture — weddings, festivals, and offerings. The variety has been cultivated in Thoubal district using traditional flood irrigation without any chemical input for over 400 years. The rice takes 45 minutes to cook and releases a distinct nutty, slightly sweet fragrance. High in zinc and iron.

name: Ladakh Apricot Kernel Oil (100ml)
description: Cold-pressed oil from apricot kernels harvested in the Nubra Valley at 3,048 metres. Ladakhi apricots grow in an extreme high-altitude desert environment without irrigation — fed only by glacial meltwater channels — and are significantly higher in oleic acid than plains-grown varieties. The kernels are solar-dried on rooftops for three weeks before cold-pressing by the Nubra Women's Alliance using a hand-cranked wooden press — no electricity used in production. Used in Ladakhi households as both a cooking oil and a skin moisturiser in extreme cold.

name: Madhubani Hand-Painted Silk Saree
description: A 6-metre Tussar silk saree painted entirely by hand by Padma Devi of Ranti village — a Maithil Brahmin woman who was awarded the National Award for crafts by the President of India in 2019. Madhubani art, practiced for over 2,500 years, traditionally decorated the walls of Mithila homes for auspicious occasions. Padma Devi uses only natural pigments: turmeric (yellow), indigo (blue), sindoor (red), carbon black (black), and rice paste (white). The design depicts the Kohbar — the bridal chamber motif — featuring fish, lotus flowers, bamboo, and the eight auspicious symbols.

name: Pochampally Ikat Cotton Kurta
description: A men's kurta woven in Pochampally double ikat technique — one of the most complex textile arts in the world, in which both warp and weft threads are resist-dyed before weaving, requiring the weaver to calculate exactly where each dyed section will intersect. The geometric pattern you see in the finished fabric is created entirely through this pre-dye calculation — no printing, no embroidery. This technique has been awarded UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage status. Cotton sourced from organic farms in Warangal, dyed with plant-based indigo and madder.

name: Kutch Bandhani Dupatta
description: A 2.5-metre Gajji silk dupatta in the Bandhani (tie-dye) tradition of Kutch, Gujarat. Made by Haleema Khatri of Bhuj whose family has practiced this craft for 14 generations. Thousands of tiny pinches of fabric are tied with thread before dyeing — each pinch tied by hand, creating a resist dot. A single dupatta of this complexity requires 60,000 individual ties and takes three weeks to complete. The pattern is a traditional Shikargah (hunt scene) with deer, flowers, and dancing figures in mustard, red, and white on a deep indigo ground.

name: Pashmina Shawl — Pure Grade A Cashmere
description: Pure Grade A Pashmina from Changthang plateau at 4,500 metres, hand-combed from Changthangi goats by nomadic herder families during the spring moulting season. The fibre diameter is 12-16 microns — finer than the finest merino wool at 18 microns. Hand-spun on a traditional yindra wooden spindle by women in Srinagar's old city, then hand-woven on a handloom. This shawl features sozni embroidery — needle-and-thread embroidery so fine that the design on the reverse is virtually identical to the front. Fully traceable from Changthang goat to finished shawl.

name: Jamdani Muslin Stole — Dhakai Design
certifications: ["GI Tag West Bengal Jamdani", "UNESCO Heritage", "Handloom Mark"]
description: A Jamdani muslin stole woven using the supplementary weft technique on a pit loom by Subrata Jana of Fulia — an inheritor of a tradition that dates back to Mughal court patronage. Jamdani is a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. The motifs — flowers, paisleys, and creepers — are created by inserting supplementary threads with a thin bamboo needle while weaving, without any pre-drawn pattern. The weaver carries the entire design in memory. The thread count is 200, making this fabric fine enough to pass through a finger ring.
